meta.description: SEE Learning System with diagnostic engine, adaptive assessment, path recommendation, and structured lessons

modes.label: Chat with AI
modes.description: Practice conversation skills with an AI partner in realistic scenarios

modes.label: Immersive VR
modes.description: Step into virtual environments for full-body language immersion

modes.label: Role Play Scenarios
modes.description: Act out real-life situations with guided dialogue and choices

modes.label: Audio Exercises
modes.description: Sharpen your listening and pronunciation with targeted audio drills

axes.label: Speaking Comfort
axes.description: How comfortable you are producing spoken English with strangers
exampleItems: Rate your comfort ordering food in English at a foreign restaurant (1=panic, 5=relaxed)
Rate your comfort introducing yourself to a group of English speakers (1=avoid, 5=enjoy)

axes.label: Listening Comprehension
axes.description: How well you understand natural spoken English in real conversations
exampleItems: You hear: 'Could you pop round to mine later?' — How well do you understand this? (1=lost, 5=clear)
Rate your understanding when watching English TV without subtitles (1=nothing, 5=everything)

axes.label: Social Speaking Anxiety
axes.description: Your anxiety level when speaking English in social situations
exampleItems: When asked to speak English unexpectedly, I feel... (1=calm, 5=intense panic)
Before a conversation in English, I rehearse what to say... (1=never, 5=always)

axes.label: Motivation Driver
axes.description: What primarily drives you to improve your English
exampleItems: I need English mostly for: [career / travel / growth / exams]
My biggest English-related goal this year is... [open text]

axes.label: Self-Efficacy
axes.description: Your belief in your ability to improve and succeed in English
exampleItems: I believe I can reach fluency if I practice regularly (1=strongly disagree, 5=strongly agree)
When I make mistakes in English, I feel... (1=devastated, 5=it's normal learning)

axes.label: Collocation Awareness
axes.description: Your intuitive grasp of natural English word pairings
exampleItems: Which sounds more natural: 'make a decision' or 'do a decision'? (score based on correctness)
Which sounds more natural: 'heavy rain' or 'strong rain'? (score based on correctness)

axes.label: Preferred Learning Format
axes.description: How you prefer to practice English
exampleItems: I learn best by... [chatting / immersing / acting / listening]
My ideal practice session looks like... [text conversation / virtual world / role play / podcast]

screeners.title: Quick Listening Check
screeners.instructions: Read the following dialogue excerpt, then answer the question below.
screeners.stimulus: A: 'Excuse me, do you know if the 42 goes to Piccadilly?' B: 'Yeah, but you'd be better off getting the Northern line from here.'
screeners.question: What does speaker B suggest?

screeners.title: Situation Anxiety Rating
screeners.instructions: Imagine you walk into a café abroad and everyone speaks English. You need to order. Rate your anxiety.
screeners.question: How anxious would you feel in this situation?

screeners.title: Natural English Check
screeners.instructions: Pick the phrase that sounds more natural in English.

screeners.title: Learning Belief Check
screeners.instructions: Rate how much you agree with the following statement.
screeners.question: I believe consistent practice will significantly improve my English within 3 months.

agents.name: DiagnosisAgent
goals: Assess learner current state from profile and diagnosticEngine
Identify anxiety level and speaking gaps

agents.name: LessonPlannerAgent
goals: Select next exercise from lesson exercises

agents.name: FeedbackAgent
goals: Generate psy-aware feedback using learner anxiety/efficacy profile
Adapt encouragement based on emotion check responses

lessons.title: Airport Small Talk
learningGoals: Initiate casual conversation at an airport gate
Manage anxiety in unpredictable social encounters

modules.title: Breathing & Grounding
modules.instructions: Before we begin, take 3 slow breaths. Remind yourself: nobody expects perfect English. The goal is connection, not perfection.

modules.title: Gate Conversation
modules.instructions: You're waiting at gate B7. The person next to you comments on the delay. Practice responding naturally. Try openers like 'Yeah, it's been a while...' or 'Do you know what's going on?'

modules.title: How Did That Feel?
modules.instructions: Rate your comfort level during the conversation. What surprised you? What would you do differently?

conversationPrompts: You're sitting at gate B7 and notice the flight is delayed by 45 minutes. The person next to you sighs and says: 'Looks like we're going to be here a while...'
The gate agent just made an announcement but you didn't catch everything. You turn to the person next to you and ask about it.
You notice the person next to you is reading a book about your favorite topic. You want to start a friendly conversation.

emotionChecks.question: How are you feeling about this conversation so far?
How confident do you feel about your English right now?

lessons.title: Café Ordering & Chat

modules.title: Perfectionism Reset
modules.instructions: Remember: native speakers don't always use 'correct' English either. Your goal is to be understood, not to be perfect.

modules.title: Counter Conversation
modules.instructions: You walk into a busy London café. The barista asks 'What can I get you, love?' Practice ordering, asking about options, and handling follow-up questions.

modules.title: Café Collocations
modules.instructions: Match the natural pairings: black coffee, flat white, sparkling water. Avoid: 'I want to have a dark coffee.'

modules.title: Confidence Check
modules.instructions: Did you feel the urge to over-prepare? Notice that and let it go. Progress over perfection.

conversationPrompts: You walk into a cozy London café. The barista greets you warmly: 'Morning! What can I get you, love?'
While waiting for your order, another customer asks you: 'Is this seat taken?' You notice they have an interesting laptop sticker about travel.
The barista calls out your order but says they're out of oat milk. They suggest an alternative.

emotionChecks.question: Did you feel pressure to order 'perfectly'?
How natural did your English feel in this situation?

lessons.title: Hotel Check-In VR
learningGoals: Handle room issues and special requests

modules.title: Arrival Confidence
modules.instructions: Visualize walking into the hotel lobby. You belong here. Take a moment to feel grounded before approaching the desk.

modules.title: Reception Desk
modules.instructions: Approach the virtual reception desk. The receptionist greets you. Practice: 'I have a reservation under [name]', handling ID requests, asking about Wi-Fi and breakfast.

modules.title: Room Issue Challenge
modules.instructions: Your room has no towels. Call reception and explain the issue politely. Practice: 'Excuse me, I noticed that...' and 'Would it be possible to...'

conversationPrompts: You've just arrived at a hotel after a long flight. The receptionist smiles and says: 'Good evening! Welcome to The Grand. Do you have a reservation?'
The receptionist asks for your passport and mentions there's a room upgrade available for a small fee.

emotionChecks.question: How comfortable are you speaking formally in English?
Would you feel confident complaining about a room issue in real life?

lessons.title: Restaurant Experience VR
learningGoals: Navigate a full restaurant experience from entry to payment
Handle menu questions and dietary requirements

modules.title: Dining Out Mindset
modules.instructions: Eating out in a foreign language feels vulnerable. Remember: waiters want to help you. They're not judging your English.

modules.title: Full Dining Experience
modules.instructions: You enter a restaurant. Practice: greeting the host, asking for a table, reading the menu, ordering, asking about specials, and requesting the bill.

modules.title: Food Collocations
modules.instructions: Learn natural pairings: 'rare steak' (not 'little-cooked steak'), 'sparkling water', 'side salad', 'house wine'.

modules.title: Social Comfort Rating
modules.instructions: How comfortable were you? Rate yourself 1-5 and note one thing you'd try next time.

conversationPrompts: You walk into a nice Italian restaurant. The host greets you: 'Good evening! Table for one, or are you expecting someone?'
The waiter brings the menu and says: 'Can I get you started with some drinks? Our house wine is particularly good tonight.'

emotionChecks.question: How anxious do you feel about dining out in English?
Could you handle an unexpected question from a waiter?

lessons.title: Job Interview Role Play
learningGoals: Manage interview anxiety with grounding techniques

modules.title: Interview Anxiety Management
modules.instructions: Interview anxiety is normal even for native speakers. Ground yourself: feet on the floor, shoulders down, 3 deep breaths. You've prepared. You're ready.

modules.title: Interview Questions
modules.instructions: The interviewer asks: 'Tell me about yourself', 'Why do you want this position?', 'What's your biggest weakness?' Practice structured answers using the STAR method.

modules.title: Unexpected Question
modules.instructions: The interviewer asks something you didn't prepare for. Practice buying time: 'That's an interesting question, let me think about that for a moment...' Then improvise an answer.

modules.title: Performance Review
modules.instructions: How did you handle the pressure? What answers felt strong? What would you refine?

conversationPrompts: You arrive at the office for your interview. The interviewer shakes your hand: 'Thanks for coming in today. Please, have a seat. So, tell me a bit about yourself.'
The interviewer looks at your CV and asks: 'Can you walk me through a challenging project you worked on?'
Near the end, the interviewer says: 'Do you have any questions for us?'

emotionChecks.question: How is your interview anxiety level right now?
Do you feel you're presenting yourself well in English?

lessons.title: Flatshare Introduction

modules.title: Social Warm-Up
modules.instructions: Meeting new people in a flat viewing is stressful in any language. Focus on being genuine rather than impressive.

modules.title: Flat Viewing Chat
modules.instructions: You arrive at a flat viewing. The current tenant shows you around. Practice: asking about the neighborhood, discussing rent and bills, talking about daily routines.

modules.title: Social Energy Check
modules.instructions: Did you feel drained or energized? Understanding your social battery helps plan real-world practice.

conversationPrompts: You ring the doorbell at a flat viewing. The current tenant opens the door: 'Hey! You must be here about the room. Come on in, I'll show you around.'
After seeing the room, the tenant asks: 'So, what do you do for work? And what's your typical day like?'

emotionChecks.question: How comfortable do you feel meeting new people in English?
Could you negotiate house rules naturally in English?

lessons.title: Doctor's Appointment

modules.title: Health Anxiety Reset
modules.instructions: Talking about health in another language is stressful. Write down key words before you start: headache, stomach, dizzy, pain. You've got this.

modules.title: Consultation
modules.instructions: The doctor asks: 'What seems to be the problem?' Describe your symptoms. Practice: 'I've been feeling...', 'It started about...', 'It gets worse when...'

modules.title: Pharmacy Follow-Up
modules.instructions: The doctor gives you a prescription. Go to the pharmacy and ask about dosage, side effects, and alternatives.

modules.title: Medical Vocab Check
modules.instructions: How confident do you feel explaining health issues in English now? What words do you still need?

conversationPrompts: You walk into the GP's office. The doctor smiles: 'Hello, take a seat. What brings you in today?'
The doctor examines you and says: 'I'd like to run a couple of tests. Have you experienced this before?'
The doctor writes a prescription: 'Take these twice a day with food. Any questions?'

emotionChecks.question: How anxious do you feel discussing health in English?
Could you describe your symptoms clearly enough?

lessons.title: Opening a Bank Account
learningGoals: Ask about account options and fees

modules.title: Financial Confidence
modules.instructions: Banking conversations have specific vocabulary. Key words: current account, savings account, direct debit, standing order, overdraft. Knowing these puts you ahead.

modules.title: Account Opening
modules.instructions: The bank advisor asks about your needs. Practice: 'I'd like to open a current account', 'Could you explain the fees?', 'What documents do I need?'

modules.title: Problem Resolution
modules.instructions: You notice an unexpected charge on your statement. Call the bank and query it politely.

modules.title: Formal Register Check
modules.instructions: Did you use appropriately formal language? Rate your comfort with financial English.

conversationPrompts: You enter the bank and approach the desk: 'Good morning, I'd like some information about opening an account, please.'
The advisor asks: 'Are you looking for a current account or a savings account? Do you need online banking?'

emotionChecks.question: How comfortable are you with formal financial conversations?
Could you handle an unexpected banking problem in English?

lessons.title: Calling Your Landlord

modules.title: Assertiveness Warm-Up
modules.instructions: Asking for things you're entitled to isn't rude—it's necessary. Practice: 'I need to report...', 'When can I expect...', 'This is quite urgent because...'

modules.title: Phone Call
modules.instructions: Your boiler has broken in winter. Call the landlord. Explain the problem, express urgency, and negotiate a repair time.

modules.title: Follow-Up Email
modules.instructions: Write a follow-up email summarizing the phone call and confirming the repair date. Practice formal written English.

modules.title: Assertiveness Check
modules.instructions: Were you firm enough? Too aggressive? Reflect on finding the balance between polite and assertive.

conversationPrompts: You dial your landlord's number. They answer: 'Hello?' You need to explain that the heating has stopped working.
The landlord says: 'I'll try to send someone next week.' You need the repair sooner because it's freezing.

emotionChecks.question: How anxious do you feel about making complaints in English?
Can you be assertive without feeling rude?

lessons.title: Returning an Item
learningGoals: Explain a problem with a purchase

modules.title: Consumer Rights Confidence
modules.instructions: Returning items is completely normal. You have the right to a refund if something is faulty. Key phrases: 'I'd like to return this', 'It's faulty', 'I have the receipt.'

modules.title: Customer Service Desk
modules.instructions: You bought a jacket online and it arrived damaged. Take it to the shop. Explain the issue and request a refund.

modules.title: Manager Escalation
modules.instructions: The staff member says they can only offer store credit. Politely ask to speak to a manager for a full refund.

modules.title: Negotiation Review
modules.instructions: Did you stand your ground? How did it feel navigating a minor conflict in English?

conversationPrompts: You approach the returns desk with a damaged jacket: 'Hi, I'd like to return this. I bought it online and it arrived with a tear.'
The staff member says: 'I'm sorry about that. Do you have your receipt or order confirmation?'

emotionChecks.question: How comfortable are you making returns/complaints?
Could you escalate politely if needed?

lessons.title: Parent-Teacher Evening

modules.title: Parent Confidence
modules.instructions: You are your child's best advocate. Even with imperfect English, your care and attention shine through. The teacher wants to help too.

modules.title: Meeting the Teacher
modules.instructions: The teacher says: 'Your child is doing well overall, but I have some concerns about...' Practice asking follow-up questions and discussing support strategies.

modules.title: Parenting in English
modules.instructions: How did it feel navigating educational English? What vocabulary do you want to strengthen?

conversationPrompts: You sit down with the teacher. They say: 'Thanks for coming in. Overall, your child is making good progress, especially in reading.'
The teacher mentions: 'I've noticed some difficulty with maths. Have you noticed anything at home?'

emotionChecks.question: How anxious do you feel about school meetings in English?
Can you discuss your child's education confidently?

lessons.title: Reporting to the Police

modules.title: Staying Calm Under Pressure
modules.instructions: Dealing with authorities in another language is stressful. Take your time. It's OK to ask them to repeat or slow down.

modules.title: Filing a Report
modules.instructions: Your bike was stolen. Go to the police station and report it. Describe: when it happened, where, what the bike looks like, any witnesses.

modules.title: Written Statement
modules.instructions: The officer asks you to write a short statement. Practice writing a clear, factual account of what happened.

modules.title: Authority Interaction
modules.instructions: How comfortable were you speaking to an authority figure in English? What would help you feel more confident?

conversationPrompts: You enter the police station. The officer at the desk says: 'Good afternoon. How can I help you?'
The officer asks: 'Can you describe the item? When and where did this happen?'

emotionChecks.question: How anxious do you feel dealing with authorities in English?
Can you provide clear descriptions under pressure?

lessons.title: Professional Networking

modules.title: Networking Mindset
modules.instructions: Everyone at networking events feels awkward—even native speakers. The trick is to ask questions. People love talking about themselves.

modules.title: The Drinks Reception
modules.instructions: You're at a conference drinks reception. Approach someone standing alone. Practice: openers, your elevator pitch, asking about their work, exchanging LinkedIn details.

modules.title: Group Conversation
modules.instructions: Join a group of three people mid-conversation. Practice entering gracefully: 'Sorry to jump in—that sounds really interesting. What are you discussing?'

modules.title: Social Battery
modules.instructions: Networking is exhausting in any language. How did you manage? What strategies worked?

conversationPrompts: You're at a tech conference. Someone near the coffee stand catches your eye: 'These events are always so packed! Have you been before?'
You strike up a conversation: 'So what brings you to the conference? Are you presenting?'

emotionChecks.question: How anxious do you feel about networking in English?
Can you pitch yourself confidently in 30 seconds?

lessons.title: Emergency Phone Call

modules.title: Emergency Readiness
modules.instructions: In an emergency, short clear sentences save lives. Practice: 'I need an ambulance', 'The address is...', 'Someone has fallen'. You don't need perfect grammar.

modules.title: The 999 Call
modules.instructions: Someone has collapsed in the street. Call 999. The operator asks: 'Which service do you need?' Practice giving your location, describing the situation, and following instructions.

modules.title: Crisis Communication
modules.instructions: How did you handle the pressure? Being able to communicate in an emergency is one of the most important language skills.

conversationPrompts: You dial 999. The operator answers: 'Emergency. Which service do you require—fire, police, or ambulance?'
The operator says: 'An ambulance is on its way. Can you tell me exactly where you are? Is the person breathing?'

emotionChecks.question: How confident are you about calling emergency services in English?
Could you give clear directions under stress?

lessons.title: Marketplace Shopping

modules.title: Market Mindset
modules.instructions: Markets are noisy and fast-paced. That's OK. You don't need perfect sentences — vendors just need to understand what you want. Relax and enjoy the experience.

modules.title: Stall Browsing
modules.instructions: You walk through a busy outdoor market. A vendor calls out: 'Fresh strawberries, three for a pound!' Practice: asking prices, quantities, and freshness.

modules.title: Haggling Challenge
modules.instructions: The vendor wants £8 for a bag. You think it's too much. Try: 'Could you do it for £5?', 'What if I buy two bags?', 'That's a bit steep for me.'

modules.title: Shopping Collocations
modules.instructions: Match natural pairings: 'ripe bananas' (not 'mature bananas'), 'loose change', 'carrier bag', 'best before date'.

modules.title: Shopping Confidence
modules.instructions: Did you feel comfortable interacting with the vendor? What phrases felt most natural? What would you try next time?

conversationPrompts: You approach a colourful fruit stall. The vendor smiles: 'Morning! What can I get you? Everything's fresh in today!'
You pick up some avocados. The vendor says: 'Those are £1.50 each, or four for £5. Lovely and ripe, ready to eat today.'
You want to buy some spices but aren't sure of the English names. The vendor asks: 'Looking for anything specific, love?'

emotionChecks.question: How comfortable do you feel shopping at markets in English?
Could you negotiate a price in English?

lessons.title: Market Sounds & Phrases
learningGoals: Recognise marketplace vocabulary in spoken form
Build listening confidence in noisy environments

modules.title: Listening in Noise
modules.instructions: Real markets are loud. You won't catch every word — and that's fine. Focus on prices, quantities, and key nouns.

modules.title: Market Conversations
modules.instructions: Read these market dialogues aloud. Customer: 'How much for a kilo of apples?' Vendor: 'Two fifty a kilo, love. Want me to bag them up?'

modules.title: Pronunciation Practice
modules.instructions: Practise these tricky market words: 'receipt' /rɪˈsiːt/, 'vegetable' /ˈvedʒtəbəl/, 'courgette' /kɔːˈʒet/, 'aubergine' /ˈəʊbəʒiːn/.

modules.title: Listening Progress
modules.instructions: Which words were hardest to pronounce? Which felt natural? Celebrate small wins.

conversationPrompts: Listen: 'Two pounds of tomatoes, a bunch of coriander, and have you got any fresh basil?' — What items are being requested?
Now practise saying: 'I'd like half a kilo of cherries and a punnet of blueberries, please.'

emotionChecks.question: How confident do you feel about your pronunciation of food items?
Can you understand prices spoken quickly?

lessons.title: Talking to Neighbours

modules.title: Diplomatic Assertiveness
modules.instructions: Good fences make good neighbours—but so does honest communication. Practice: 'I hope you don't mind me mentioning...', 'I wondered if we could...'

modules.title: The Knock on the Door
modules.instructions: Your upstairs neighbour has been playing loud music late at night. Knock on their door and address it calmly.

modules.title: Conflict Resolution
modules.instructions: Did you find the right tone? Reflect on how you balanced being polite with being heard.

conversationPrompts: You knock on your neighbour's door. They open it: 'Oh, hello! Everything alright?'
Your neighbour says: 'Oh, I'm so sorry! I didn't realise the music was that loud. I'll keep it down.'

emotionChecks.question: How comfortable are you raising issues with neighbours?
Can you be assertive without creating conflict?

lessons.title: Podcast Comprehension Drill
learningGoals: Understand main ideas from natural speech
Build tolerance for not understanding every word

modules.title: Imperfect Listening
modules.instructions: You don't need to understand every word. Even native speakers miss things. Focus on the main message, not every detail.

modules.title: Listen & Respond
modules.instructions: Read the transcript excerpt of a conversation between two friends planning a weekend trip. Then answer: What are they planning? When are they going? What's the disagreement about?

modules.title: Spoken Collocations
modules.instructions: From the conversation, identify natural pairings: 'catch a train', 'grab a bite', 'pop into a shop'. Practice using them in your own sentences.

conversationPrompts: Listen to this excerpt: 'Sarah: I was thinking we could catch the early train on Saturday. Mike: Saturday? I thought we said Sunday.' What are they discussing?
Now try to use the phrases you heard in your own sentence about weekend plans.

emotionChecks.question: How frustrated do you feel when you don't understand everything?
Do you believe your listening will improve with practice?

lessons.title: Pronunciation & Intonation
learningGoals: Practice problematic sounds for your L1
Build confidence in your spoken delivery

modules.title: Accent Acceptance
modules.instructions: Your accent is part of your identity. The goal isn't to sound native—it's to be clearly understood. Every accent is valid.

modules.title: Minimal Pairs Practice
modules.instructions: Practice distinguishing and producing: ship/sheep, bat/bet, think/sink. Read each pair aloud and focus on the difference.

modules.title: Record & Compare
modules.instructions: Record yourself saying: 'I'd like to book a table for two, please.' Listen back. Is the meaning clear? That's what matters.

modules.title: Voice Comfort
modules.instructions: How did it feel hearing your own English? Rate your comfort 1-5. Remember: discomfort decreases with practice.

conversationPrompts: Let's practice together. Say this sentence out loud: 'I'd like to book a table for two, please.' Which sounds were hardest for you?
Try these minimal pairs: 'ship' vs 'sheep', 'bat' vs 'bet'. Which pair do you find most challenging?

emotionChecks.question: How do you feel about hearing your own voice in English?
Do you think your pronunciation is clear enough to be understood?

lessons.title: Dictation & Spelling Bee

modules.title: Spelling Anxiety
modules.instructions: English spelling is notoriously difficult—even for native speakers. 'Necessary', 'accommodate', 'definitely'—everyone struggles. Be patient with yourself.

modules.title: Dictation Round
modules.instructions: Read each sentence aloud, then write it down from memory: 'The weather forecast said it would be sunny, but I brought my umbrella just in case.' Check your spelling carefully.

modules.title: Tricky Words
modules.instructions: Spell these commonly misspelled words: 'separate', 'occurred', 'recommend', 'occasionally', 'embarrassed'. Check yourself and note patterns.

modules.title: Spelling Patterns
modules.instructions: Did you notice any patterns in the words you misspelled? Double letters? Silent letters? This awareness speeds up improvement.

conversationPrompts: Let's try a dictation exercise. Read this and then write it from memory: 'I've been meaning to ask you something.'
Now try a harder one: 'The restaurant we went to last Wednesday was absolutely brilliant.'

emotionChecks.question: How do you feel about your English spelling?
Do you avoid writing in English because of spelling?

lessons.title: Rhythm & Word Stress

modules.title: Rhythm Matters
modules.instructions: English is a stress-timed language—some syllables are loud and long, others are quick and quiet. Getting the rhythm right often matters more than individual sounds.

modules.title: Stress Pattern Drill
modules.instructions: Mark the stressed syllable: phoTOgrapher, photoGRAPHic, PHOtograph. Notice how stress shifts. Say each word aloud emphasizing the stressed syllable.

modules.title: Sentence Stress
modules.instructions: Say: 'I didn't say HE stole the money.' Now shift emphasis: 'I didn't say he STOLE the money.' How does meaning change? Practice with 3 more sentences.

modules.title: Rhythm Awareness
modules.instructions: Can you hear the 'music' of English now? Rate your awareness 1-5. This is one of the most powerful improvements you can make.

conversationPrompts: Let's play with word stress. Say these three: PHOtograph, phoTOgrapher, photoGRAPHic. Can you feel the stress moving?
Now try sentence stress: 'I NEVER said that' vs 'I never SAID that'. What changes?

emotionChecks.question: How aware are you of English rhythm and stress?
Do you think improving rhythm will help your speaking?

lessons.title: Connected Speech & Linking

modules.title: Why Can't I Understand Fast English?
modules.instructions: Native speakers don't say 'What do you want to do?' — they say 'Whaddya wanna do?' This isn't lazy—it's natural connected speech. Learning to decode it is a superpower.

modules.title: Decoding Drill
modules.instructions: Decode these connected speech examples: 'Djeet yet?' = 'Did you eat yet?', 'Wanna cuppa?' = 'Do you want a cup of tea?', 'Gonna head out' = 'Going to go outside.'

modules.title: Natural Speed Practice
modules.instructions: Say these at natural speed, linking the words: 'turn_it_off', 'pick_it_up', 'look_at_it'. The underscore shows where words connect.

modules.title: Comprehension Shift
modules.instructions: Has understanding connected speech changed how you hear English? Rate the difference before and after this exercise.

conversationPrompts: Can you decode this? 'Aye dunno, whatcha reckon?' Hint: 'I don't know, what do you reckon?'
Try saying these naturally: 'Not at all' → 'Notatall'. 'Would you like a cup of tea?' → 'Wudja likeacuppatea?'

emotionChecks.question: How frustrated do you get when native speakers talk fast?
Do you feel you're improving at decoding fast speech?

lessons.title: Learn Through Songs

modules.title: Music & Memory
modules.instructions: Songs activate emotional memory—one of the most powerful ways to remember vocabulary. When you sing along, you're training pronunciation, rhythm, and vocabulary simultaneously.

modules.title: Gap-Fill Challenge
modules.instructions: Read this lyric excerpt with blanks: 'Is this the real life? Is this just ___? Caught in a ___, no escape from ___.' Fill in the missing words. (Queen - Bohemian Rhapsody)

modules.title: Sing & Learn
modules.instructions: Pick a song you love. Find the lyrics online. Read them first, then try to sing along. Note 3 new words or phrases you discovered.

modules.title: Emotional Connection
modules.instructions: How does learning through music feel compared to textbook exercises? Does it make vocabulary more memorable?

conversationPrompts: Let's use music! Can you complete this lyric: 'Yesterday, all my ___ seemed so far away...' (The Beatles)
What's your favourite English song? Can you share a lyric and explain what it means to you?

emotionChecks.question: Do you enjoy learning English through music?
Has a song ever helped you remember a word or phrase?

exercises.prompt: The person next to you says 'Terrible delay, isn't it?' — respond naturally.
Your flight is boarding. End the conversation politely.
Order a flat white and a blueberry muffin. The barista asks 'For here or to go?'
Choose the natural phrase: 'black coffee' or 'dark coffee'?
You arrive at the hotel. Tell the receptionist you have a reservation and ask about breakfast times.
Your room doesn't have hot water. Call reception and explain the problem politely.
Ask the waiter about today's specials and order a main course with a side.
Ask for the bill, check if service is included, and pay by card.
The interviewer says 'Tell me about yourself.' Give a 30-second professional introduction.
'What's your biggest weakness?' Answer using the growth framing technique.
Introduce yourself to your potential new flatmate. Mention your job, hobbies, and daily routine.
Your flatmate suggests a 'no guests after 10pm' rule. Negotiate politely.
Describe these symptoms to a doctor: headache for 3 days, feeling dizzy, no appetite.
Ask the pharmacist about side effects and how often to take the medication.
Ask the bank advisor about the difference between a current account and a savings account.
You notice a £25 charge you don't recognise. Call and query it politely.
Your boiler is broken. Call the landlord and explain you need it fixed urgently.
Return a faulty item and request a full refund. The staff offers store credit instead.
Ask the teacher how your child is doing in reading and what you can do to help at home.
Report a stolen bicycle. Describe: colour, make, where and when it was taken.
Introduce yourself and your work in 30 seconds at a professional networking event.
Call 999 and report that someone has collapsed in the street. Give your location clearly.
Tell your neighbour about the noise problem without creating conflict.
After reading the transcript, summarize the main topic in one sentence.
Use 'catch a train' and 'grab a bite' in your own sentences about weekend plans.
Say aloud and distinguish: 'ship' vs 'sheep', 'bat' vs 'bet'. Type which pair you find hardest.
Read aloud: 'I'd like to book a table for two, please.' Focus on rising intonation for politeness.
Write this from memory: 'The weather forecast said it would be sunny, but I brought my umbrella just in case.'
Spell correctly: separate, occurred, recommend, occasionally, embarrassed.
Mark the stressed syllable: photograph, photographer, photographic. Say each aloud.
Say 'I didn't say HE stole the money' with different stress. How does meaning change?
Decode: 'Djeet yet?' What does this mean in full English?
Say these naturally at speed: 'turn it off', 'pick it up', 'look at it'. Link the words together.
Fill the gaps: 'Is this the real life? Is this just ___? Caught in a ___, no escape from ___.'
Pick your favourite English song. List 3 new words or phrases you learned from the lyrics.

adaptivityRules.description: If learner reports high anxiety mid-session, insert a psy_prep module
If self-efficacy is low, add encouragement module

avatarCharacters.description: Professional airport gate agent who handles delays and boarding procedures.
Friendly London café barista with a warm, chatty personality.
Professional but approachable interviewer at a UK company.
Friendly existing tenant showing a prospective flatmate around.
Polite hotel front-desk staff who handles check-ins and guest requests.
